schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read Daniel as simply a second name for the Chileab of 2 Samuel 3:3 (not a copyist's error), and supply the cross-references for Amnon, Ahinoam, and Abigail.
Gill notes the six Hebron sons stand in the same order as the older record and that Daniel here is the Chileab of Samuel, one man with two names.
Matthew Henry lifts the list into praise of David's house, the most illustrious in Israel, from which Christ came; Nathan named for the prophet, and from him our Lord descended (Luke 3:31).
Clarke gathers the Targum's 'like to his father' (ke-le-ab) and Jarchi's paternity-doubt explanation of the double name Daniel/Chileab.

outliers.note: Relays Jarchi's tradition that the two names answered a suspicion after Nabal's death — Daniel, 'God is my judge, I have not fathered another's child,' and Chileab, 'like to the father.'

themes: the sons of David born at Hebron

schools.summary: Gill identifies Bathshua with Bathsheba and her father Ammiel with Eliam, names of like signification, and notes nine sons in all were born at Jerusalem.

Clarke explains Bath-shua/Bath-sheba as a copyist's exchange of letters and Solomon's last place by his bearing the line; Wesley presses that all four were Bathsheba's, Solomon her 'only son' (Proverbs 4:3) only in her love.
Ellicott notes the concubines' sons go unnamed, Tamar is named for her famous sorrow (2 Samuel 13), and the list's independent form suggests it was not drawn from Samuel.
Gill takes the summary plainly: these were all David's sons by his wives, the concubines' children set apart.
Clarke marks Tamar the only daughter of David on record, though the history says he had both sons and daughters (2 Samuel 5:13).

Matthew Henry reads the royal line as the recompense of David's piety — the unbroken descent from Solomon (and of Nathan, Luke 3) alone related to the Messiah — and looks ahead to the interrupted succession and to Anani the hoped Messiah (Daniel 7:13).
Ellicott unlocks the royal names as confessions of faith: Rehoboam, Abijah ('Iah is father'), Asa ('healer'), Jehoshaphat ('Iahweh judgeth').
Gill notes vv.10-14 reckon David's successors in the order of their reigns down to Josiah.

Ellicott marks the end of primogeniture with Josiah, identifies Shallum with Jehoahaz (2 Kings 23:30), Jehoiakim with Eliakim, Zedekiah with Mattaniah, and explains the order by reign-length and full brotherhood (Hamutal's sons).
Wesley adds the common identification of Shallum with Jehoahaz (2 Kings 23:30).

themes: the glory departing from David's house

schools.summary: Clarke reads 'son' as 'successor' if this be the last king Zedekiah; Wesley calls it 'another Zedekiah' and muses on the crown passing nephew to uncle, a presage of departing glory.
Gill holds this cannot be the last king (Jeconiah's uncle, not brother) but another, otherwise-unknown Zedekiah, son of Jehoiakim.
Ellicott keeps 'his son' in its plain sense — a younger Zedekiah named after his great-uncle who never reigned — and identifies Jeconiah with Jehoiachin/Coniah (Jeremiah 22:24).

debates.question: Is 'Zedekiah his son' the last king of Judah (named as successor) or a distinct, otherwise-unknown son?

sides.position: The last king Zedekiah, 'son' meaning 'successor' on the throne
A distinct younger Zedekiah who never reigned; 'son' in its natural sense

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'the sons of Jeconiah when captive — Shealtiel his son,' reading Assir as 'captive' per the Masoretic pointing; Keil and Delitzsch note Luther took Assir as the appellative 'prisoner,' not a name.
Clarke doubts Assir was a person, reading it as the 'prisoner' epithet of Jeconiah (Salathiel his son, Matthew 1:12); Wesley reads 'of Jechoniah the captive,' begetting his son in Babylon.
Gill resolves the 'childless' of Jeremiah 22:30 — Jeconiah had children but none to succeed him on the throne.

debates.question: Is 'Assir' the name of a son of Jeconiah, or the appellative 'captive' describing Jeconiah?

sides.position: The appellative 'captive/prisoner' — Jeconiah begot Shealtiel in exile
A personal name, a son Assir
holders: the Talmud tract Sanhedrin (as reported by Ellicott)

themes: the exile stamped on the names

schools.summary: Ellicott takes the six as sons of Jeconiah and brothers of Shealtiel, reads Shenazar as a Babylonian name ('Sin protect,' cf. Belteshazzar, Daniel 1:7) and Hoshama as 'the LORD hath heard'; Keil and Delitzsch treat the block as the families carrying David's blood through exile.
Clarke (with Calmet) and Wesley supply 'the sons of Salathiel were Malchiram and Pedaiah,' making the six Shealtiel's sons rather than his brothers.

themes: the brothers or sons of Shealtiel

schools.summary: Gill offers three harmonizations of Zerubbabel's double parentage: Pedaiah a son of Salathiel; or Salathiel's adoption of Zerubbabel; or a levirate marriage raising seed to the childless Salathiel.
Wesley holds Zerubbabel the proper son of Pedaiah and grandson of Salathiel; Clarke reports Houbigant's deletion of 'the sons of Pedaiah' (wanting in Arabic/Syriac) to agree with Matthew 1:12.
Ellicott keeps the Hebrew: Zerubbabel son of Pedaiah, nephew of Shealtiel, reconciled if adopted as Shealtiel's heir; names him the prince who led the first exiles home under Cyrus.

debates.question: Was Zerubbabel the son of Pedaiah (as here) or of Shealtiel (as Haggai, Ezra, and Matthew 1)?

sides.position: Son of Pedaiah in fact; Shealtiel's fatherhood is by adoption or the levirate
Son of Shealtiel; delete 'the sons of Pedaiah' with the Arabic and Syriac

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch carry the six-son list forward as drawn unaltered from David's history; Ellicott notes the 'to Absalom'/'Absalom' variant, Kimchi's LO-ABSHALOM ('not Absalom') wordplay, and Adonijah put to death by Solomon (1 Kings 2:19-25).

Ellicott denies the repeated names are an error — David gave such faith-filled names to sons of different wives; Keil and Delitzsch set the thirteen Jerusalem sons against 1 Chronicles 14 and the eleven of 2 Samuel 5:14-16.
Clarke gives both views (mistake, or sons dying young and renamed); Wesley holds the second, that names of the dead were given to later sons to preserve their memory.

debates.question: Are the twice-occurring names Elishama and Eliphelet a scribal error or deliberate?

sides.position: Two earlier sons of these names died young and later sons received their names
No error — David deliberately gave such expressive names to sons of different wives

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the steady 'his son' formula and the deliberate omission of Athaliah, who was no descendant of David; Ellicott glosses Joram, Ahaziah ('Iah holdeth,' cf. Luke 1:54), and Joash.

themes: the covenant kept through dark reigns

schools.summary: Clarke reports the rabbinic identification of Eglah with Michal but objects from Michal's childlessness (2 Samuel 6:23); Wesley takes 'his wife' to mark Eglah as David's first and most proper wife.
Ellicott calls the identification of Eglah with Michal groundless; 'his wife' merely balances the clause where the mother's family details are lacking.

debates.question: Was Eglah, mother of Ithream, the same person as Michal the daughter of Saul?

sides.position: Yes — Eglah is a byname of Michal, Saul's daughter
No — the identification is groundless and clashes with Michal's lifelong childlessness

outliers.note: Takes Eglah for David's first and 'most proper' wife, set last only because her son was born last — neither Michal nor a mere balancing name.

themes: wives named for the sons' sake

schools.summary: Clarke reckons the numbers (thirteen with Bathsheba's four, nine without; Samuel's seven or eleven) and, with Jarchi, holds the Chronicler counts the dead as well as the living; Wesley agrees only seven stand in 2 Samuel 5:14.
Ellicott traces Eliada/Beeliada to the old use of Baal ('lord') for God, later replaced by El, Iah, or bosheth ('shame'), as Ishbosheth for Eshbaal.
Clarke holds a name has dropped out unless the father is counted; Wesley offers that the father Shemaiah makes up the six, or that 'Shisha' (six) is itself a son's name.
Ellicott takes one name lost by oversight, and identifies Hattush with the companion of Ezra (Ezra 8:2-3), four generations after Zerubbabel.

debates.question: Why does the text say 'six' sons of Shemaiah but name only five?

sides.position: A name has dropped out by a copyist's oversight
The father Shemaiah is counted in the six; or 'Shisha' (six) is itself a son's name

themes: a name lost or the father counted
the line traceable in the return

schools.summary: Ellicott dates the sons of Elioenai to the sixth generation from Zerubbabel, about 345 B.C. under Artaxerxes Ochus, and uses it to fix the date of the whole book.

Clarke reports the Targum and Jarchi that Anani is 'the King Messiah who is to be revealed' (from the 'clouds,' ananey, of Daniel 7:13) but calls the reasoning fanciful, noting the Syriac/Arabic drop names to make twenty-three verses.
Matthew Henry (via Bishop Patrick) reports the same Anani-Messiah gloss and, though calling it far-fetched, takes it as proof the Jews' minds were full of the Messiah, expected soon after Zerubbabel.

outliers.voice: the Targum (as reported by Clarke and Matthew Henry)
outliers.note: Reads the chapter's last name, Anani, as 'the King Messiah who is to be revealed,' finding the word in the 'clouds' (ananey) of Daniel 7:13 — a reading both Clarke and Henry judge far-fetched yet telling of Jewish messianic expectation.

themes: the whole line bending toward the coming King

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the Hebrew 'Six were born' against Samuel's 'These were born,' and that the regnal notice (seven and a half years at Hebron, thirty-three at Jerusalem), absent from the Samuel parallel, is drawn from 2 Samuel 2:11 and 2 Samuel 5:5.

Ellicott reads Amaziah ('Iah is strong'), Azariah ('Iah helpeth'), and Jotham ('Iahweh is perfect'); Keil and Delitzsch's note that the kings are named in strict order, each 'his son,' governs the verse.
Matthew Henry's reflection presides: the crown's long direct descent, rare among nations, was the recompense of David's piety, God keeping covenant through generation after generation.

themes: the line preserved despite the men

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Ahaz (abbreviation of Jehoahaz), Hezekiah ('my strength is Iahu'), and Manasseh (perhaps Egyptian); the succession follows Keil and Delitzsch's 'his son' formula down to Josiah.
Matthew Henry's thought that the unbroken descent was the recompense of David's piety holds even over faithless Ahaz and idolatrous Manasseh — the promise rested on God, not on any single heir.

themes: the sharpest swings of Judah's faith

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses Amon (Egyptian sun-god) and Josiah ('Iah comforteth') and counts fifteen consecutive monarchs with Athaliah omitted; Keil and Delitzsch mark that the regular succession ceases with Josiah.
Matthew Henry's admiration of the crown's long direct descent, the recompense of David's piety, reaches its last unbroken link at Josiah.

themes: the last king to reign in peace

schools.summary: Ellicott takes the five as a second group of Zerubbabel's children, notes the lost 'and' before Jushab-hesed, and reads their names as prophecies of restoration hope — Meshullam, Ohel ('tent'), and Jushab-hesed ('mercy shall be restored,' cf. Habakkuk 3:2) against the last kings' names of judgment (Isaiah 10:22).

themes: the second group of Zerubbabel's sons
the theology of the returned remnant

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs the LXX/Syriac descending-chain variant ('his son' for 'sons') but keeps the harder Hebrew, taking 'the sons of Hananiah' to gather in the four following families as brothers or subdivisions.
Wesley reads all — parents and sons together — as the sons of Hananiah and branches of the royal stock.

themes: the royal stock spreading in obscurity

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Elioenai as 'unto Iah are mine eyes' (cf. Psalms 123:1-2; Psalms 25:15) and notes an Elioenai who went up with Ezra (Ezra 8:4), matching the dim genealogy to a man of the return.

themes: a name matched to the return
the waiting hope of David's house
